summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Summary Dismissal – Where the applicant filed an Initiating Application which sought orders in almost identical terms to the applicant in Vasquez & Vasquez [2026] FedCFamC1F 16 (“Vasquez & Vasquez”) – Where the Court made an order under s 102QAC of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) in Vasquez & Vasquez – Where the respondent in this matter was also the respondent in Vasquez & Vasquez – Where the respondent asserted that present application is a “front” pursued by the applicant in Vasquez & Vasquez – Where the respondent sought that the applicant’s case be summarily dismissed – Where the respondent sought that a costs order be made against the applicant– Where the Court finds that the application is frivolous – Where the Court finds that the application is doomed to fail – Where the Court summarily dismisses the application – Where the Court makes an order for costs against the applicant on an indemnity basis.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALTOBELLI J:

INTRODUCTION

These reasons for judgment explain the orders made by this Court on 28 November 2025 as between the Applicant (“the maternal grandmother”) and the Respondent (“the father”) (collectively “the parties”) concerning two children, X born 2009 and Y born 2012 (collectively “the children”). This matter was heard simultaneously with Vasquez & Vasquez [2026] FedCFamC1F 16 (“Vasquez & Vasquez”), where the mother of the children was the applicant (“the mother”) and the father of the children was the respondent (collectively “the parents”).
Vasquez & Vasquez related to proceedings which the mother commenced and later discontinued. The Court made an order under s 102QAC of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) and a costs order on an indemnity basis against the mother. The father contends that the current proceedings commenced by the maternal grandmother are frivolous and are merely an opportunity for the mother to advance her own case before the Court, despite the effect if the s 102QAC order. The Court agrees.

BRIEF BACKGROUND

The maternal grandmother is seventy-two years old and is a retired health worker. The information available in relation to the maternal grandmother is scanty. The accompanying affidavit filed with the maternal grandmother’s Initiating Application filed on 24 September 2025 (“the maternal grandmother’s application”) has been completed by the maternal grandfather, who is not a party to the proceedings. Doing the best it can, the Court infers that the maternal grandmother also “resides in [Town J] UK and also Australia every year” alongside the maternal grandfather (Affidavit of [Mr K] filed 24 September 2025 (“the maternal grandfather’s affidavit”, paragraph 2).
The maternal grandmother’s application lists her address for service in Australia as the same residential address attributable to the mother in Vasquez & Vasquez. On this basis, whilst visiting Australia, the Court infers that the maternal grandmother resides with the mother in Suburb B. The father was born in New Zealand, is forty-nine years old, and is a professional at L Company. The father and the children reside in rental accommodation in Suburb B.

PROCEDURAL HISTORY

On 24 September 2025, the maternal grandmother filed her application. She sought a raft of ancillary final orders such that the children’s passports are to be held by her, for the children’s school to provide the parents with all information and documents requested, and for non-denigration orders. Additionally, the maternal grandmother sought to discharge all previous final orders, save for ancillary orders and travel related orders, between the parents which were made by Schonell J by consent on 18 June 2024 (“the final orders between the parents”). Lastly, she sought that the children live between the father and the maternal grandmother, as agreed between the parties.
On 24 October 2025, I made orders in Chambers which listed the maternal grandmother’s application for hearing to consider whether or not the Court ought to summarily dismiss her application. I directed the maternal grandmother to file and serve an affidavit which address this point. Notification was provided in my orders which confirmed that the Court intended to hear this matter simultaneously with Vasquez & Vasquez. The maternal grandmother did not file any material in accordance with my orders.
On 12 November 2025, I made orders which listed the father’s Application in a Proceeding filed 31 October 2025 (“the father’s costs application”) and required the maternal grandmother to file and serve a response to his application, a supporting affidavit, a financial statement and written submissions. The maternal grandmother did not file any material in accordance with my orders.
On 28 November 2025, I made final orders and now I provide these reasons in explanation for those orders.

THE EVIDENCE

The maternal grandmother did not file any material in relation to whether or not the Court should summarily dismiss her application. Further, the maternal grandmother did not appear at the listing to prosecute her application.
In support of his orders sought, the father relied on the following evidence:
Written Submissions filed 27 November 2025 (“the father’s Written Submissions”);
His Affidavit filed 14 October 2025 including all annexures therein (“the father’s affidavit”);
His Affidavit filed 31 October 2025 (“the father’s costs affidavit”);
Application in a Proceeding filed 31 October 2025 (“the father’s costs application”);
The father’s Proposed Minute of Orders provided to Chambers on 28 November 2025;
The father’s Proposed Minute of Orders handed up on 28 November 2025 (“the father’s proposal”);
Child Impact Report prepared by Court Child Expert, Ms E dated 25 September 2025 (“the Child Impact Report”); and
Documents tendered during the hearing which became Exhibits A1–A2.

THE PARTIES PROPOSALS

The maternal grandmother did not advance a formal position in relation to whether the Court ought to summarily dismiss her application, despite being provided with the opportunity to do so.
It is apposite to acknowledge that the substantive final orders sought in the maternal grandmother’s application are almost identical to those sought by the children’s mother in Vasquez & Vasquez, save for an additional order that the children ought to live with the maternal grandmother and father as agreed between the parties (the maternal grandmother’s application, Order 23). The Court observes two impracticalities with Order 23 of the maternal grandmother’s application; firstly, when in Australia the maternal grandmother resides with the mother, and secondly, it is unclear if the maternal grandmother proposes that the children should live with her during any time she is residing in the United Kingdom. The maternal grandmother’s application failed to particularise with adequate specificity her proposal for the time she ought to spend with the children.
The father sought that the maternal grandmother’s application be summarily dismissed and for a costs order to be made on an indemnity basis in the amount of $861.30.

THE APPLICABLE LAW

Summary dismissal

When considering whether to summarily dismiss an application, the Court is guided by Part XIB of the Act. The Explanatory Memorandum to the Family Law Amendment Bill 2023 (Cth) at paragraph [310] sets out that:
Division 1A contains new section 102QAB. This section substantially replicates previous section 45A, which is repealed by item 1. Section 102QAB brings the text in line with current drafting practice and clarifies the language to improve usability.
Relevantly, s 102QAB of the Act provides:
Summary decrees

No reasonable prospect of successfully defending proceedings
The court may make a decree for one party (the first party) against another in relation to the whole or any part of proceedings if:
the first party is prosecuting the proceedings or that part of the proceedings; and
the court is satisfied that the other party has no reasonable prospect of successfully defending the proceedings or that part of the proceedings.

No reasonable prospect of successfully prosecuting proceedings
The court may make a decree for one party (the first party) against another in relation to the whole or any part of a proceedings if:
the first party is defending the proceedings or that part of the proceedings; and
the court is satisfied that the other party has no reasonable prospect of successfully prosecuting the proceedings or that part of the proceedings.

When there is no reasonable prospect of success
For the purposes of this section, a defence or proceedings or part of proceedings need not be:
hopeless; or
bound to fail;

to have no reasonable prospect of success.

Proceedings that are frivolous, vexatious or an abuse of process
The court may dismiss all or part of proceedings at any stage if it is satisfied that the proceedings or the part is frivolous, vexatious or an abuse of process.
To avoid doubt, proceedings or a part of proceedings are not frivolous, vexatious or an abuse or process merely because an application relating to the proceedings or the part is made and later withdrawn.

Costs
If the court makes a decree, or dismisses all or part of proceedings, under this section, the court may make such order as to costs as the court considers just.

Action by court on its own initiative or on application
The court may take action under this section on its own initiative or on application by a party to the proceedings.
The test to be applied is made clear in s 102QAB(2), that is “there is no reasonable prospects of success”. Section 102QAB(3) clarifies that the Court need not find that the application is “hopeless” or “bound to fail” as was previously required (see Bigg & Suzi [ 1998] FamCA 14). Indeed, the full Court endorsed in Gong & Zao [2021] FamCAFC 110 (“Gong & Zao”) at [15] that:
...the present test is a “different enquiry” to the earlier one, “it is dangerous to seek to elucidate the meaning of the statutory expression ‘no reasonable prospect of successfully prosecuting the proceeding’ by reference to what is said in those earlier cases” (Spencer v The Commonwealth (2010) HCA 28; (2010) 241 CLR 118 per Hayne, Crennan, Kiefel and Bell JJ at [56]).
Nevertheless, the Full Court confirmed that the cautions against too readily summarily disposing of proceedings as expressed by Kirby J in Lindon v The Commonwealth (No. 2) [1996] HCA 14; (1996) 136 ALR 251 at [255]–[256] remain relevant (Gong & Zao at [16]).
Pierson & Romilly [2025] FedCFamC1F 148 helpfully sets out at [10]–[11] that:
The principles in dealing with summary dismissal applications can be found in Bigg & Suzi [ 1998] FamCA 14 and Beck & Beck [2004] FamCA 92 (“Beck”). Those are: the summary dismissal power is discretionary; it is rarely and sparingly provided; the parties seeking summary dismissal must show the application is doomed to fail, or their opponent lacks a reasonable cause of action, or is advancing a claim that is clearly frivolous or vexatious.

A weak case or one unlikely to succeed is not sufficient to warrant termination. If a serious legal question is to be determined, it should ordinarily be determined at a trial. If, notwithstanding defects of pleadings, it appears a party may have a reasonable cause of action which they have failed to put in proper form, the Court should allow the party to reframe proceedings. The High Court has referred to the need to exercise exceptional caution in the exercise of the power and that it should never be exercised unless it is clear there is no real question to be tried. Webster & Lampard [1993] HCA 57; (1993) 177 CLR 598 at [602], which was approved in Beck in this Court. The Court is required to take the applicant's case at its highest (Lindon & Commonwealth [1996] HCA 14)

Costs

The power to order costs is conferred upon the Court by Part XIVC of the Act. The starting position is clear, that is, that each party should bear their own costs (s 114UB(1)). Subsection 114UB(2) provides that if the Court is of the opinion that there are circumstances that justify an order for costs, it may. The parameters of this Court’s power in this respect are found in s 114UB(3), s 114UC and s 114UD of the Act and the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”). The discretion to award costs is a “broad” one, and the various enumerated factors are not to be read in any restrictive way (Collins and Collins [1985] FamCA 15; (1985) FLC 91-603).
When considering making a costs order, the Court must have regard to the following factors as outlined in subsection 114UB(3):
(3) In considering what costs order (if any) should be made, the court must have regard to the following:
(a) the financial circumstances of each party to the proceedings;
(b) whether any party to the proceedings is receiving assistance by way of legal aid in respect of the proceedings and, if so, the terms of the grant of the assistance to that party;

(c) the conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings including, without limiting paragraphs (a) and (b), the conduct of the parties in relation to pleadings, particulars, discovery, inspection, directions to answer questions, admissions of facts, production of documents and similar matters, and in relation to their duty of disclosure under subsection 71B(1), 90RI(1) or 90YJA(1);

(d) whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the court;

(e) whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings;

(f) whether a party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle the proceedings and the terms of any such offer;

(g) such other matters as the court considers relevant.
It is well settled that no single factor in s 114UB(3) has priority, nor must more than one factor be satisfied. Indeed, any one factor may be sufficient (Fitzgerald (as child representative for A (Legal Aid Commission of Tasmania)) v Fish and Anor [2005] FamCA 158; (2005) 33 Fam LR 123). However, the matters considered “must all be taken into account and all balanced in order to determine whether the overall circumstances justify the making of an order for costs” (I and I (No 2) [1995] FamCA 80; (1995) FLC 92-625 at 82,277).

DISCUSSION

The father’s case

The father’s overarching argument in relation to the dismissal of the maternal grandmother’s application is that such application is “simply a front for the Mother to obtain and advance her case before the Court” (father’s written submissions, paragraph 70.2). There were additional arguments advanced by the father at paragraph 70 of his written submissions. Such arguments were expressed in a more refined manner in oral submissions.
During submissions the father contended that the Court ought to find that the maternal grandmother’s application is deficient for the following reasons:
The maternal grandmother did not file an affidavit in support of her application;
The maternal grandmother has not participated in any form of alternative dispute resolution in accordance r 4.01 of the Rules;
The reasons advanced by the maternal grandmother in her Genuine Steps Certificate filed 24 September 2025 as to why an exemption from the requirement to pursue alternative dispute resolution prior to filing (the matter was urgent and a previous family law application had been filed by one of the parties in the last 12 months), were not supported on any factual basis by the maternal grandmother’s materials; and
The ‘spend time with’ interlocutory orders sought by the maternal grandmother in her Application in a Proceeding filed 24 September 2025 (“the maternal grandmother’s AIAP”) are without basis or merit, and are frivolous in circumstances where she primarily resides in the United Kingdom and is “subject to a[n Australian] non-working visa which is limited to a maximum of 3 months per year” (father’s costs affidavit, paragraph 3).
Separately, the father also contended that the maternal grandmother’s application formed a backdoor attempt to advance the mother’s position. The father submitted:
The address for service and the email address filed on the maternal grandmother’s application are identical to that on material filed in support of the mother’s case in Vasquez & Vasquez;
The final orders sought in the maternal grandmother’s application are almost identical to those sought by the mother;
The maternal grandmother’s AIAP is almost identical to the Application in a Proceeding filed by the mother on 16 June 2025 in Vasquez & Vasquez;
Orders 16 and 20 of the maternal grandmother’s AIAP appear to show the word “grand” is inserted by way of handwritten amendment to correct the orders to read “grandmother” instead of “mother”. The implicit contention within this proposition being that the handwritten amendments support the notion that the mother rebadged her own application before the Court behind the shield of a different name; and
The suspicious timing of the maternal grandmother’s application. Her application was, curiously, filed on 24 September 2025. This is prior to the mother discontinuing her applications before the Court; 2 October 2025 and 17 October 2025 (which were the subject of judgment in Vasquez & Vasquez). By inference the father suggested that the maternal grandmother’s application was filed to act in support, or indeed substitution, of the mother’s discontinued applications.

Ruling on the father’s case

Since the filing of her application and accompanying documents, the maternal grandmother has failed to file any further material despite being ordered to do so on 24 October 2025 and 12 November 2025. This renders her in default according to r 10.26(1)(a) of the Rules. A possible consequence for being in default under the Rules is “dismissal as to the whole or any part of the relief claimed by the party” (r 10.27(1)(a)). Further, given the maternal grandmother’s non-attendance, it is open for the Court to dismiss her application in its entirety (r 15.19(1)(c)).
From a more substantive perspective, it is also open for the Court to suggest given the absence of any oral submissions, on the material filed in support of the maternal grandmother’s case alone, that the maternal grandmother has no reasonable prospect of successfully prosecuting the proceedings.
Despite the seriousness of the contention, the Court is comfortably satisfied that the maternal grandmother’s application serves as a “front” for the mother’s interests. There are many pieces of evidence which go to this finding. Many are outlined above. For example, the maternal grandmother’s address for service is identical to the mothers. Whilst this does not alone prove that the maternal grandmother is the front of a “proxy” application, given the s 102QAC order in place against the mother, the Court has no confidence that any emails alleged to be sent by the maternal grandmother will not be, or already have not been, subject to the influence of the mother. The whole purpose of a s 102QAC order is to prevent harm to litigants and children. It would make a mockery of the provision and the protection it provides to allow materially similar applications to be filed ad nauseum differentiated only by the name of the pseudo main applicant. Additionally, in the circumstance of this matter, the Court has no confidence that any of the material filed by the maternal grandmother was filed on her own volition.
The Court is of the view that the orders sought by the maternal grandmother in her application are frivolous. No bona fide applicant would have sought the extraordinary orders she sought in the manner that she has sought them. The basis for the orders sought are not explained, they are contrary to existing final orders by consent made between the parents, and even if made, would pose significant practical difficulty, if not impossibility, in implementation. If the application is a “front” for the mother, then the application on its face it frivolous. If the maternal grandmother’s application is genuine then it is not merely a weak case, but one doomed to fail. In either event, there is sufficient basis for the Court to summarily dismiss the action brought by the maternal grandmother.
Of relevance to the genuineness of the maternal grandmother’s application is the fact that X recently identified to Court Child Expert, Ms E that “his father had to block the maternal grandmother’s number, due to increasing messages and calls, however, [X] shared his thoughts that it is his mother messaging and calling his father, rather than the maternal grandmother” (Child Impact Report dated 25 September 2025 (“the Child Impact Report”), paragraph 12). Additionally, at paragraph 14 of the Child Impact Report, X stated that:
... he feels unsafe when his mother continues to come to their house to see him. He stated that since the maternal grandparents have been in Australia, they will arrive to his home unannounced, with his mother waiting in the car, while the maternal grandmother makes attempts to get [X] and [Y] to return to the care of their mother.
Whilst the Court is not in a position to forensically test the veracity of what X said, the Court does not view his narrative as contradictory to any other evidence before the Court.
Taking the maternal grandmother’s case at its highest, I am satisfied that she does not have a reasonable prospect of success. The mother and father have current final orders of this Court governing their parenting arrangements. The application advanced by the maternal grandmother has numerous procedural failings, without even considering the matter that the legal substance of her application was barren. The maternal grandmother did not address any factor set out in s 65DAAA of the Act. This is the only method under the Act by which a Court may reconsider final parenting orders. The Court chooses to view that the maternal grandmother’s application was both frivolous and unrealistic and therefore warrants summary dismissal.

Costs

The father sought an order that the maternal grandmother pay his costs on an indemnity basis calculated to be $861.30, of an incidental to, the maternal grandmother’s application. Details concerning the costs incurred by the father are found in his Costs Notice filed 28 November 2025. These costs are manifestly reasonable on their face.
Where an order is sought for costs to be made on an indemnity basis, the Court must determine “whether the overall circumstances justify the making of an order for costs” (I and I (No 2) at 82,277) and “whether the particular facts and circumstances of the case in question warrant the making of an order for payment of costs other than on a party/party basis” (Colgate Palmolive Co v Cussons Pty Ltd [1993] FCA 536; [1993] 46 FCR 225 at [257]).
The father’s argument in relation to the conduct of the maternal grandmother that warrants a costs order is set out at paragraph 70 of his written submissions. The maternal grandmother was required to file responsive material to this and was provided with ample notice of the listing date in which the father’s costs application would be considered. Indeed, the father produced a letter from his legal representatives dated 4 November 2025 which was sent to the maternal grandmother serving his application, his affidavit and a copy of my orders made 3 November 2025 (Exhibit A1). Despite being on sufficient notice of the listing, the maternal grandmother elected not to defend the father’s cross-application.
No argument was advanced that could suggest that a costs order on an indemnity basis was not entirely reasonable. The bare fact of the Court summarily dismissing the maternal grandmother’s application demonstrates that any legal costs spent fighting the maternal grandmother’s application were wholly unnecessary, and that the maternal grandmother was wholly unsuccessful in the matter. The Court accepts the written submissions of the father at paragraph 70.
Even if the maternal grandmother claimed impecuniosity, the Court would still make the costs order sought by the father. The Court has considered the factors expressed in s 114UB(3) and the Rules and is satisfied that an indemnity costs order is just in the circumstances of this matter.
